scenario: A new developer joining the team wants to set up a local Camunda 8 environment on their laptop to develop and test BPMN processes before deploying to the team's dev cluster. They need an Orchestration Cluster (Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist + Identity) plus the Connectors runtime. They also want the local environment to be lightweight (laptop-friendly resource usage) and easy to tear down/restart. (Web Modeler is NOT part of either local quickstart bundle -- Desktop Modeler is the recommended local modeler.) The senior architect mentions there are two main options: Camunda 8 Run and Docker Compose. Both are documented as developer-quickstart paths.
question: What is the correct understanding of these two options?

options.text: Camunda 8 Run is a deprecated option; only Docker Compose is supported. md:13-21. It was introduced as a streamlined alternative to Docker Compose for developers who want a single command to bring up a complete local environment. md), Camunda 8 Run is fully supported. 10) but not Camunda 8 Run.

That claim is false on both counts. Camunda 8 Run and Docker Compose developer quickstart are explicitly designed and documented for local development, not production use. Production Self-Managed deployments use Kubernetes (Helm charts or Kubernetes Operator), which provides the cluster management, scaling, high availability, and operational tooling that Camunda 8 Run and Docker Compose deliberately omit for simplicity. The SaaS Free Tier is an alternative for developers who prefer cloud-hosted development, but it is not the only or recommended option -- both local quickstart paths are valid and well-documented.

exe start) that brings up the Orchestration Cluster (Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, Identity) + Connectors in one process. md:38; default secondary storage is H2. It's the lightest option for local development. yml file -- more configurable but heavier. Both are supported developer-quickstart paths. Web Modeler is NOT bundled in either; use Desktop Modeler locally.

Camunda 8 Run requires a Kubernetes cluster; Docker Compose is the only Docker-only option. This is a double error. md:38 -- it is a JVM-based bundled distribution that runs all Camunda 8 components in a single JVM process using H2 as the default storage backend. It explicitly does not require Kubernetes, Docker, or any container runtime. Docker Compose, by contrast, does require Docker. Both options run on a developer laptop without any container orchestration or Kubernetes infrastructure. The two options differ in their runtimes (JVM vs Docker containers), not in their infrastructure requirements.

a.text: Incorrect. Both are supported developer-quickstart paths in Camunda 8 per c8run.md:13-21. Camunda 8 Run is a single bundled Camunda distribution that brings up the Orchestration Cluster (Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, Identity) + Connectors + H2 default storage in one CLI command per c8run.md:23-27; prerequisite is OpenJDK 21-25 per c8run.md:38 (NOT "Java 17" -- the docs explicitly require 21+); start via ./start.sh / ./c8run start on Mac/Linux or .\c8run.exe start on Windows per c8run.md:64-80. Docker Compose runs the same components as separate containers linked via docker-compose.yml -- more configurable (per-component scaling, networking customisation), but heavier resource-wise; can also be launched via the c8run wrapper with ./c8run start -docker to pick relational-DB presets per c8run.md:107-120. The choice is operational: Camunda 8 Run for "one command, everything running" simplicity; Docker Compose for "I want container isolation and per-service configurability". Web Modeler is NOT in either local-quickstart bundle.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 Run is actively supported and recommended for the simplest developer experience per c8run.md:13 (8.9-alpha referenced; 8.8 GA fully supports c8run).

c.text: Correct. Local dev with Camunda 8 Run or Docker Compose is supported and common; SaaS Free Tier is an alternative but not the only option.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 Run requires Java (no Kubernetes); Docker Compose requires Docker (no Kubernetes either). Both run on a developer laptop without K8s.

explanation: Local dev with Camunda 8 Run or Docker Compose is supported and common; SaaS Free Tier is an alternative but not the only option.
